common.title: Controlled Switch
common.description: Demonstrates a controlled Switch component where its state is managed by React's `useState` hook.

common.title: Switch with Label
common.description: Shows how to properly associate a `Switch` with a label using `htmlFor` and `id` for accessibility.

common.title: Disabled Switch
common.description: An example of a Switch component rendered in a disabled state, preventing user interaction.

minimal.title: Basic Uncontrolled Switch
minimal.description: A simple uncontrolled switch without any explicit state management, useful for basic toggles.

response.description: The Switch component is a UI element designed for toggling between two distinct, binary states, typically "on" and "off". It provides an intuitive way for users to enable or disable features, settings, or preferences that take effect immediately without the need for an explicit save action. Built upon Radix UI primitives, it ensures high accessibility and customizable styling. Common use cases include activating dark mode, enabling notifications, or toggling feature flags within an application. Its immediate feedback mechanism makes it ideal for direct manipulation of state.

guidance.accessibility: Ensure the Switch is always associated with a `<label>` element using `htmlFor` and `id` for screen reader accessibility. The component should be keyboard navigable using `Tab` and activatable using `Space` or `Enter`. Radix UI base ensures correct ARIA roles and attributes (e.g., `role="switch"`, `aria-checked`). Provide clear visual focus indicators.
guidance.whenNotToUse: Do not use a Switch when a user action requires confirmation or an explicit save, such as submitting a form; prefer a Checkbox with a Button in such cases. Avoid using it for initiating an action that doesn't toggle a state (use a Button instead). Do not use for selections with more than two states (consider Radio Buttons or a Select component).
guidance.whenToUse: Use the Switch component for binary settings that trigger an immediate state change without requiring a separate save action. Examples include toggling a dark mode theme, enabling/disabling notifications, activating a feature directly, or showing/hiding UI elements.
